text: Management of a severe stricture after sleeve gastrectomy, conversion to gastric bypass. The patient is a thirty nine year old female. Her past medical history is significant for gastroesophageal reflux disease and anemia. The past surgical history is significant for laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy in The patient complains of severe reflux and insufficient weight loss.

The patient underwent an EGD and an upper GI, where a narrowing was identified at the level of the gastric pouch, resulting in a proximal dilatation of the stomach. The patient decided to undergo a robotic-assisted conversion from sleeve gastrectomy to Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, resection of the stricture.

The surgical setup includes a camera port positioned to the left of the umbilicus, with two robotic ports in the left flank and left upper quadrant. An additional robotic trocar is placed in the right upper quadrant for the robot's right arm, and an assistant trocar is positioned between the camera arm and the right robotic port.

The surgeon begins the procedure by lysing adhesions between the previous sleeve gastrectomy and the liver. All visible adhesions are meticulously removed from the sleeve, revealing a dilatation in the proximal part of the sleeve.
The surgeon creates a window posterior to the sleeve and transects proximal to the stricture, forming the gastric pouch. The stricture is visibly identified during this process.
The surgeon uses a stapler to transect the sleeve distal to the stricture, effectively resecting the stricture at the level of the pylorus.
The surgeon retracts the transverse colon to identify the Treitz's angle. Approximately thirty centimeters distal to this point, the jejunum loop is transected.
The surgeon measures and runs the alimentary limb approximately 120 centimeters before performing a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy using a stapler.
The surgeon advances the alimentary limb cranially and performs a gastrojejunostomy, beginning with a running posterior suture using 3-0 PDS suture material.
After completing the outer-posterior layer, the surgeon opens the gastric pouch and creates an enterotomy using a monopolar hook, revealing the interior of the pouch.
The surgeon performs an inner-posterior layer suture using 3-0 PDS, reinforcing the gastric pouch with precise stitching.

The surgeon begins the anterior aspect of the anastomosis using a running 3-0 PDS suture. A second running suture is initiated from the opposite side, meeting the first suture in the middle, where they are tied together for improved reconstruction.
The surgeon completes the inner and anterior layer of the anastomosis and proceeds to perform a second layer on top using a running suture.
The operating time was two hundred and two minutes. The estimated blood loss was fifty centiliters. No complications were registered.
A postoperative upper GI showed no leaks and the anastomosis was permeable. The patient was discharged on the postoperative
